content.text: In a conventional software project, success criteria are stated as acceptance tests: does the feature behave as specified? For Claude integrations, this framing is insufficient. Claude's outputs are not deterministic — two valid responses to the same question may differ in phrasing, structure, and detail while both being correct. Success criteria for Claude integrations must be linked to an evaluation framework that can handle this variability.

Eval-linked success criteria define what the system must achieve in measurable, testable terms, and specify how it will be evaluated. They have three components: a quality bar (the numeric threshold the system must meet), an evaluation protocol (how the system's outputs are scored against that bar), and a test set (the inputs used to generate outputs for scoring). Together they form the specification that the system prompt engineer, evaluator, and business stakeholder all agree on before the system is built.

A success criterion without an evaluation method is a preference, not a requirement. 'The system should be accurate' is a preference. 'The system achieves accuracy >= 90% on the 100-query evaluation set, scored by human reviewers using the attached rubric' is a requirement.
The Eval Pipeline: Discovery to Continuous Evaluation

attrs.alt: Diagram of the eval-linked pipeline from discovery phase through eval design, pre-deploy gate, and continuous production evaluation with feedback loop

content.text: Eval-linked success criteria exist at every stage of the Claude integration lifecycle. They are first defined during discovery, operationalised into a test suite during the build phase, applied as a deployment gate before go-live, and used for continuous evaluation in production. The feedback loop from production evaluation back to the success criteria is what keeps the specification grounded in observed system behaviour.

Every eval-linked success criterion should define three quality bar levels, not one. A single threshold creates a binary pass/fail that does not support nuanced programme decisions. Three levels — minimum, target, and stretch — give the steering committee the information they need to make differentiated decisions:

The floor below which the system must not be deployed. A system below the minimum bar poses unacceptable risk — it will mislead users, generate compliance problems, or damage the use case's credibility. This is a hard deployment gate.
The threshold that corresponds to the promised business case. Reaching the target bar means the programme has delivered what was committed. This is the number the steering committee tracks.

An aspirational threshold that, if met, would justify further investment or expanded scope. Reaching stretch does not happen automatically — it requires deliberate architectural improvements and is used as a trigger for a programme expansion conversation, not a routine target.

These example values are starting references, not universal standards. The correct quality bar values depend on the consequences of errors in the specific integration. A contract review assistant where missed issues create legal liability has a higher minimum bar than a knowledge base Q&A used for internal reference.

The evaluation protocol specifies exactly how outputs will be scored. An underspecified protocol produces inconsistent scoring — two evaluators will reach different accuracy figures from the same set of outputs — which makes the quality bar meaningless as an acceptance criterion. A complete protocol defines:

Human scoring, LLM-as-judge, automated string matching, or a combination. Human scoring is most reliable but expensive; LLM-as-judge scales but requires a validated meta-evaluation against human scores.

Binary (correct / incorrect), ordinal (fully correct / partially correct / incorrect), or continuous (0–5 scale). The choice affects the interpretation of the quality bar threshold and must be consistent with how the bar is stated.

What specific criteria make an output 'correct'? For factual accuracy: is the claim verifiable against the source knowledge base? For issue identification: was every material issue identified, or only critical issues? Rubric ambiguities become inter-rater disagreements at scale.

For human scoring, how are disagreements between evaluators resolved? A 2-of-3 majority rule, an appeal to a domain expert, or a separate adjudication pass all produce different results and must be specified in advance.
Pre-deployment (mandatory), post-deployment monthly sample (ongoing), and ad hoc after system prompt changes (triggered). The protocol must be runnable on the cadence specified.

The test set is the collection of inputs used to generate outputs for scoring. Its composition determines what the quality bar actually measures — and therefore what it does not measure. A poorly constructed test set can produce a quality bar that looks good while the system fails on real-world queries.

Queries should reflect the actual distribution of inputs the system will receive in production. If 60% of real queries are straightforward factual lookups and 30% are multi-part analytical questions, the test set should reflect this split.
Deliberate coverage of known edge cases:

Beyond the main distribution, include queries that are known to be difficult: ambiguous queries, out-of-scope queries, adversarial inputs, queries involving unusual named entities, and queries at the boundary of the system's knowledge.
Ground truth sourced from domain experts:

The expected correct answer for each query must be established by a domain expert, not inferred by the prompt engineer. Ground truth established by the person who built the system introduces confirmation bias into the evaluation.

A test set of fewer than 50 queries produces accuracy estimates with large confidence intervals. For a binary metric, a sample of 100 queries gives ±10% confidence bounds; 400 queries gives ±5%. The minimum practical test set is 100 queries; 200–500 is the standard range for pre-deployment evaluations.

The test set must be versioned and frozen for comparison purposes. If queries are added or removed between evaluations, the scores are not comparable. Changes to the test set should be tracked with the same discipline as code changes.

Human evaluation at the scale required for continuous production monitoring is expensive and slow. LLM-as-judge — using a separate Claude call to score outputs against the rubric — scales to thousands of evaluations per day. The approach is valid when the judge model has been calibrated against human scores, but requires careful setup to avoid systematic biases.
A judge that has not been calibrated against human evaluators cannot produce a score that corresponds to the quality bar as stakeholders understand it. Calibration requires:
Score 100–200 test cases with human evaluators first, recording the score and reasoning for each.
Run the same 100–200 cases through the LLM judge prompt. Compare the judge's scores to the human scores case by case.

Calculate inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa or percentage agreement). A kappa > 0.7 indicates the judge is reliably replicating human scoring; < 0.6 indicates the rubric or judge prompt needs refinement.
For cases where judge and human diverge, revise the judge prompt to address the source of disagreement — typically ambiguous rubric criteria or examples missing from the judge's context.
Re-calibrate after each rubric change. A calibration is specific to the rubric version it was built on.

# LLM-as-judge eval runner with calibration check
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

JUDGE_SYSTEM = """You are evaluating outputs from an AI contract review assistant.
Your task is to score each output against the ground truth issues identified by legal experts.

Scoring criteria:
- Score 1 (correct): The assistant identified all critical issues and at least 80% of material issues.
- Score 0.5 (partial): The assistant identified all critical issues but fewer than 80% of material issues.
- Score 0 (incorrect): The assistant missed one or more critical issues.

Return JSON: {"score": <0|0.5|1>, "reasoning": "<one sentence explanation>"}
"""

def judge_case(assistant_output: str, ground_truth_issues: list[dict]) -> dict:
    prompt = f"""Assistant output:
{assistant_output}

Ground truth issues:
{ground_truth_issues}

Score the assistant output using the provided rubric."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=256,
        system=JUDGE_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )

    import json as _json
    return _json.loads(response.content[0].text)

def run_eval(cases: list[dict], integration_output_fn) -> dict:
    scores = []
    for case in cases:
        output = integration_output_fn(case["input"])
        result = judge_case(output, case["ground_truth_issues"])
        scores.append(result["score"])
        print(f"{case['case_id']}: {result['score']} — {result['reasoning']}")

    mean_score = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    return {
        "n": len(scores),
        "mean_score": round(mean_score, 3),
        "passed_minimum": mean_score >= 0.75,
        "passed_target": mean_score >= 0.88,
    }
Linking Eval Results to Deployment Decisions

The eval-to-deployment link is the mechanism that gives the quality bar operational meaning. Without a formal process connecting eval results to go/no-go decisions, the quality bar is advisory and will be ignored under schedule pressure.
Deploy to production; move to continuous eval cadence
Deploy with explicit time-bound plan to reach target bar; steering committee review at 30 days
Root cause analysis; system prompt revision; re-eval before any deployment
Identify what changed (system prompt, knowledge base, model version); revert if cause unclear

The deployment gate is only as strong as the organisational will to enforce it. A quality bar that has been overridden once under schedule pressure will be ignored in every subsequent evaluation. Make the gate process visible to the steering committee — not just to the programme team.

A single accuracy score collapses multiple quality dimensions into one number, losing information that is critical for diagnosing failures. A contract review assistant with an overall score of 87% might be scoring 97% on critical issue identification but only 72% on minor issue identification. If the minimum bar for critical issues is 90% and the programme team is tracking the aggregate score, they may not notice that the critical issue dimension has breached its threshold.

The solution is to define separate quality bars for each important quality dimension and track them independently. Multi-dimensional quality bars allow targeted diagnosis: if one dimension falls while others hold, the prompt engineer knows exactly which capability needs attention. Common dimensions:
Are stated facts verifiable against the source knowledge base?
Human review against knowledge base; binary per claim
Are all relevant items identified, not just some?
Human review against ground truth list; percentage of items found
Are flagged items actually relevant, or are there irrelevant items mixed in?
Human review of flagged items; percentage of flags that are valid
Does the response address the actual question asked?
Human or LLM judge on 1-5 relevance scale
Does the response follow the required output format?
Does the response match the specified persona and communication style?
Human or LLM judge against tone rubric
Does the response contain claims that cannot be grounded in provided context?
Human or LLM judge per claim; percentage ungrounded

Not every integration needs all seven dimensions. Decide which dimensions are critical for the specific use case and define quality bars only for those. An information extraction integration (structured output from unstructured text) cares intensely about precision and recall but may not care about tone consistency. A customer service integration may care intensely about tone and relevance but have a softer accuracy bar because responses are reviewed by a human before delivery.
Writing the Success Criteria Specification Document

The eval-linked success criteria should be captured in a specification document that is formally reviewed and signed off by the BU lead, the system architect, and the compliance or legal owner before the build phase begins. The document has five sections:

One paragraph describing the integration, the user population, and the use case. This section anchors the success criteria to a specific business context and prevents later scope creep from invalidating the quality bars.
Table of each quality dimension, its definition, measurement method, minimum bar, target bar, and stretch bar. Signed off by BU lead.
Scoring method, rubric (full text or reference), evaluator selection criteria, adjudication process, and evaluation cadence. Signed off by system architect.

Composition rules (category counts, distribution targets), ground truth sourcing method, versioning approach, and current version number. The test set itself is attached as an appendix or referenced by file path.
The decision matrix linking eval results to deployment decisions. Named individual who has authority to approve conditional deployment. Signed off by BU lead and compliance owner.

The success criteria specification is not a design document — it does not describe how the system will work. It describes what the system must achieve and how that will be verified. Keep these two concerns in separate documents; mixing them produces a document that is neither a good specification nor a good design.
Common Failure Modes in Eval Design

Evaluation frameworks for Claude integrations fail in recurring patterns. Knowing these patterns during discovery allows the requirements team to design them out before they become problems at evaluation time:

The prompt engineer builds the system prompt by working through the test set, inadvertently optimising for the test set rather than real-world performance. Separate the team that builds the test set from the team that builds the system prompt. The test set should be held in a separate repository with restricted access during the build phase.
Ground truth set by the wrong people:

Ground truth established by the prompt engineer who built the system produces circular evaluation — the system achieves high scores on ground truth that reflects its own limitations. Domain experts who will use the system must establish ground truth, not the team that built it.
A single aggregate accuracy score hides which dimensions are failing. Always score multi-dimensionally and report each dimension separately.

A test set built at the time of initial deployment does not account for the queries the system actually receives in production. After 30 days of production use, review the real query log and update the test set to include query types that were not anticipated. Set up a quarterly test set review process.

An LLM judge that scores 87% agreement with human evaluators on the calibration set may still systematically disagree on specific query categories. Review calibration results by category, not just in aggregate. A judge that agrees overall but disagrees on adversarial cases will produce misleading scores for the evaluation's most important test cases.
Quality bar set without reference to baseline:

A quality bar of 90% sounds rigorous. But if human evaluators on the same task achieve 93%, the bar is below human performance and the integration is not meeting the standard it needs to meet. Always establish human baseline performance on the test set during the discovery phase and set quality bars relative to that baseline.
A quality bar that is met at deployment does not remain met indefinitely. Three categories of change can degrade quality without any deliberate system change:

Documents added to the knowledge base may introduce conflicting information, lower-quality content, or topics that confuse the retrieval system. A knowledge base that grows from 500 documents to 5,000 documents over 12 months is a materially different system from the one that passed the pre-deployment eval.

Users find new ways to use the system that were not anticipated in the test set. A support assistant trained on product FAQ queries will receive integration-specific technical questions as users become more sophisticated. The test set no longer represents the real query distribution.

Anthropic releases model updates that change default behaviour. A system built on claude-sonnet-4-6 may behave differently on claude-sonnet-5-0. Pinning to a specific model version delays this effect but requires active migration when the pinned version is deprecated.

The continuous eval cadence is the primary defence against quality regression. A monthly sample of 50–100 production queries, scored against the quality bar using the same protocol as the pre-deploy eval, will detect regression before it becomes a user-visible problem. Alert thresholds at 80% of the minimum bar give the programme team warning time to investigate before the bar is breached.

# Continuous eval: sample production queries and score monthly
import anthropic, random

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def sample_production_queries(query_log: list[dict], n: int = 100) -> list[dict]:
    # Stratified sample to match test set composition
    standard = [q for q in query_log if q["category"] == "standard"]
    edge     = [q for q in query_log if q["category"] == "edge_case"]
    sample = (
        random.sample(standard, min(70, len(standard))) +
        random.sample(edge, min(30, len(edge)))
    )
    return sample[:n]

def run_monthly_eval(query_log: list[dict], integration_fn, judge_fn) -> dict:
    sample = sample_production_queries(query_log, n=100)
    scores = []
    for q in sample:
        output = integration_fn(q["input"])
        score = judge_fn(output, q.get("reference_answer"))
        scores.append(score)
    mean = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    return {
        "n": len(scores),
        "mean_score": round(mean, 3),
        "minimum_bar": 0.80,
        "target_bar": 0.92,
        "status": "ok" if mean >= 0.80 else "BREACHED",
        "alert": mean < 0.84,  # alert at 80% of minimum bar = 0.80 * 1.05
    }

Every quality bar should be set relative to measured human baseline performance on the same task. Without a human baseline, there is no way to know whether the quality bar represents improvement over the status quo, parity with human performance, or a lower standard than the team that Claude is replacing. Three baseline comparison outcomes are possible, each with different programme implications:
The integration is performing better than the pre-integration human process on the measured dimension. This is the ideal outcome — it justifies the investment and supports expanding scope.

The integration matches human performance. This outcome is valuable if the business case was cost reduction (Claude at the same quality level as humans, but cheaper or faster) rather than quality improvement.

The integration is performing worse than the human process it is replacing on the measured quality dimension. This outcome requires a programme decision: is the shortfall in a dimension that matters for the use case? If yes, the integration should not be deployed to replace the human process until the quality gap is closed.

Establishing the human baseline requires running a representative sample of the test set through the human process and scoring the human outputs against the same rubric used for Claude outputs. The practical steps: give 20–50 test cases to 2–3 domain experts, score their outputs using the evaluation rubric, and calculate the human mean score and variance. This data becomes the reference point against which Claude's pre-deployment eval scores are compared.
Conditional deployment; prompt engineering work needed to close gap
Full deployment; Claude exceeds human baseline
Conditional deployment with human review of low-confidence extractions
Conditional deployment; knowledge base quality improvement underway

A Claude integration that matches or exceeds human baseline performance on the critical quality dimensions, and is faster or cheaper to operate, has a strong business case that is easy to defend. An integration that falls below human baseline requires a more nuanced business case — faster, cheaper, but lower quality — and requires explicit stakeholder agreement that the quality reduction is acceptable for the use case.

In regulated industries, the quality bar and evaluation protocol may have compliance implications beyond internal programme management. Financial services, healthcare, legal, and public sector organisations may be required to demonstrate that AI-assisted decisions meet a documented standard of accuracy, that evaluation is performed by qualified reviewers, and that evaluation records are retained for audit purposes.
During discovery, identify whether the integration falls under any regulatory framework that imposes requirements on AI evaluation. Common frameworks and their implications:

Requires documented accuracy requirements, human oversight protocols, and post-market monitoring plans before deployment. The eval-linked success criteria specification serves as the core of the technical documentation required under the Act.

Consumer Duty requires that AI-assisted customer communications are fair, clear, and not misleading. Quality bars for response accuracy and relevance should be set with reference to the Consumer Duty standard, and the evaluation protocol should be documented as part of the firm's Consumer Duty evidence pack.

Clinical decision support tools in the United States that meet the definition of a medical device require FDA clearance. Quality bars for accuracy in clinical contexts must be aligned with the FDA's Software as a Medical Device guidance, which sets more stringent evidence standards than internal programme management.

Organisations operating under an ISO 9001-certified quality management system must integrate the eval framework into their documented quality procedures. The test set, rubric, and eval results become quality records subject to the retention and audit requirements of the QMS.

Eval-linked success criteria connect business goals to testable quality bars. The quality bar has three levels (minimum, target, stretch) each with different decision implications. The evaluation protocol specifies scoring method, rubric, and evaluator process. The test set is constructed to cover the real distribution of queries plus deliberate edge cases, with ground truth from domain experts. LLM-as-judge enables continuous evaluation at scale but requires human calibration before it can produce valid scores. Deployment is gated on the pre-deploy eval, and production monitoring uses the same framework to detect regressions before they become visible business problems.
